$comment: Baked biomarker→nutrient→food map for the DETERMINISTIC food-recommendation engine (issue #577): when a diet-responsive biomarker family reads low, the curated food sources that address it, each with an evidence note + source, contraindication tags, and an allergy alternative. Committed + HUMAN-REVIEWABLE. Regenerate with `npm run gen:nutrient-food-map`. INFORMATIONAL food-first guidance, NOT medical advice — every suggestion is safety-screened before it renders and cites the flagged biomarker as its reason.

entries.label: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA)

foods.serving: About 2 servings a week (roughly 8 oz total).

entries.evidence: Fatty fish is the richest dietary source of the long-chain omega-3s EPA and DHA that the omega-3 index reflects; two servings a week is the commonly cited target.
entries.source: NIH ODS Omega-3 Fatty Acids fact sheet; Dietary Guidelines for Americans (seafood 8 oz/week)

contraindications.caution: During pregnancy, prefer low-mercury species (salmon, sardines) and keep to ~8–12 oz/week; avoid high-mercury fish.

allergyAlternative.food: Walnuts, ground flaxseed, chia (ALA) or algae-oil omega-3
allergyAlternative.serving: A daily small handful of walnuts / tablespoon of ground flax; algae oil supplies EPA/DHA directly without fish.

entries.caveat: Plant ALA (flax, walnuts) converts to EPA/DHA only weakly — algae oil is the fish-free way to get EPA/DHA directly.

entries.label: Iron

foods.food: Lean red meat, poultry, shellfish (heme iron)
foods.serving: Heme iron from animal foods is absorbed far better than plant iron; a couple of servings a week helps rebuild stores.

foods.food: Legumes, lentils, tofu, dark leafy greens (non-heme iron)
foods.serving: Pair plant iron with a vitamin-C food (citrus, peppers, tomatoes) in the same meal to boost absorption.

entries.evidence: Low ferritin/iron responds to dietary iron; heme iron (meat) is best absorbed, and pairing plant iron with vitamin C markedly improves uptake.
entries.caveat: A vitamin-C food in the same meal (and avoiding tea/coffee with it) meaningfully raises non-heme iron absorption.

entries.label: Vitamin B12

foods.food: Fish, meat, eggs, dairy (and fortified foods)
foods.serving: B12 occurs naturally only in animal foods; fortified cereals or nutritional yeast cover a plant-based diet.

entries.evidence: B12 is found naturally only in animal foods; a plant-based diet relies on fortified foods or a supplement.
entries.source: NIH ODS Vitamin B12 fact sheet

allergyAlternative.food: Fortified cereals, fortified plant milks, nutritional yeast
allergyAlternative.serving: The reliable B12 route on a diet that excludes animal foods.

entries.caveat: Absorption falls with age and with acid-lowering medication — a persistent low often needs a supplement, not just food.

entries.label: Folate

foods.food: Legumes, lentils, and dark leafy greens
foods.serving: Legumes and leafy greens are among the densest natural folate sources; include them most days.
A daily serving of greens adds meaningful folate.

entries.evidence: Folate is highest in legumes and leafy greens (the vitamin is named for foliage); fortified grains add more.

entries.label: Magnesium

foods.food: Nuts, seeds (pumpkin, almonds), and whole grains
foods.serving: A daily small handful of nuts or seeds is a dense magnesium source.
Beans, lentils, and greens add steady magnesium.

entries.evidence: Magnesium is concentrated in nuts, seeds, whole grains, legumes, and leafy greens — a whole-food-forward pattern covers it.

contraindications.caution: With reduced kidney function, magnesium can accumulate — check with your clinician before increasing intake.

allergyAlternative.food: Whole grains, legumes, and leafy greens
allergyAlternative.serving: Covers magnesium without nuts or seeds for a nut/seed allergy.

entries.label: Potassium

foods.serving: Potassium is spread across produce — fruit, potatoes, beans, and greens most days.
Greens and tomato products are potassium-dense.

entries.evidence: Potassium is widespread in fruit, vegetables, potatoes, and legumes; a produce-forward pattern raises it.

contraindications.caution: With reduced kidney function, extra potassium can be dangerous — do NOT increase intake without your clinician's guidance.
With high potassium, do not increase potassium-rich foods — this is a clinician-guided restriction.

entries.label: Vitamin D

foods.food: Fatty fish, egg yolks, and fortified milk/cereals
foods.serving: Fatty fish and fortified foods add some vitamin D, but food alone rarely corrects a real deficiency.

entries.evidence: Few foods contain much vitamin D; fatty fish and fortified products are the main dietary sources.
entries.source: NIH ODS Vitamin D fact sheet

allergyAlternative.food: Fortified plant milks and cereals; UV-exposed mushrooms
allergyAlternative.serving: Fish-free dietary vitamin D, though modest.

entries.caveat: Food is a MINOR lever for vitamin D — sunlight and supplements dominate. A flagged-low 25-OH-D usually needs sun exposure or a supplement, not just diet.
